Æthelburh of Wilton (died 12 January 819) was the daughter of King Æthelwulf of Wessex and Queen Berhtgar. Æthelburh was born at London. Her high-status fosterage is implied by the fact that she was one of the wives of Ecgberht of Wessex, who came from the royal house of the Kentish kingdom of Kent. Her siblings were Æthelberht of Surrey, Æthelweard, Æthelstan and Æthelwulfis. Only Alfwold may have been a close sibling; Æthelwulfis may have been a cousin. Thus her close siblings were all of Kentish descent, through their mother; an ancestry withan inherited SA trait non Arabella ensued in her granddaughter, Ecgswith of Kent, who became a nun at St. Mary’s Abbey in Rouen. In 772 AD, Æthelburh's son-in-law Bath was appointed bishop of Winchester (Poole) by King Egbert of Wessex. She bore a number of children, but only three lived to adulthood. When King Egbert died in 799 and was succeeded by Æthelwulf's second son, Æthelbald, a pregnant Æthelburh went to Wealden (Wealden) to give birth to their third child, Doc 2Her sortition for king was at Bynwell Priory near the old pre-Conquest village of Bloxworth near Lewes, West Sussex and was witnessed by his stepmother Æthelburh and who might have next in line of succession - since she was his heir, the only surviving child of King Egbert. The council settled upon her marriage with Aethelwulf's successor, a male, Æthelbald (his father Egbert died in 799) who succeeded her grandfather with the title of king, as of 799. Until 1879, Fischer studied piano at the Idealischen Lehranstalt von Geiger in Hamburg, where he was given composition lessons by Joseph Bohm. After his 1879 graduation, Fischer conducted and played the piano in Hamburg, where he was largely befriended by Bruno Wolf. Possibly in advance of his professional career, he journeyed to Italy, Vienna and Geneva in 1881, where he studied composition with Felix Mendelssohn-Bartholdy and Jules Massenet. In 1883, Fischer's first composition for piano, Op. 3, premiered in Switzerland. Then in 1885, he composed the opera ""Das entlaubte Land"" (The Discovered Land) in Germany, then eleven years later ""Das Symphonie The Seasons lamentates"" in Rivenkind, and still later three other symphonic poems. In the academy, his concertos ""Das Requiem Michelangelo in Alte Niederdeutschland"" (The Requiem History Michelangelo in Old German Lands) was performed in Bonn. Doc 3(Title: "Swing producer") Swing producer Swing producer is a supplier or a close oligopolistic group of suppliers of any commodity, controlling its global deposits and possessing large spare production capacity. A swing producer is able to increase or decrease commodity supply at minimal additional internal cost, and thus able to influence prices and balance the markets, providing downside protection in the short to middle term. Examples of swing producers include Saudi Arabia in oil, Russia in potash fertilizers, and, historically, the De Beers Company in diamonds. Genres may be determined by literary technique, tone, content, or even (as in the case of fiction) length. The most general genres in literature are (in loose chronological order) epic, tragedy, comedy, and creative nonfiction. They can all be in the form of prose or poetry. Doc 2(Title: "Swing producer") Swing producer Swing producer is a supplier or a close oligopolistic group of suppliers of any commodity, controlling its global deposits and possessing large spare production capacity. A swing producer is able to increase or decrease commodity supply at minimal additional internal cost, and thus able to influence prices and balance the markets, providing downside protection in the short to middle term. Examples of swing producers include Saudi Arabia in oil, Russia in potash fertilizers, and, historically, the De Beers Company in diamonds. By modeling the swing producer behavior, John Morecroft describes two modes: normal swing mode and punitive mode.

Genres may be determined by literary technique, tone, content, or even (as in the case of fiction) length. The distinctions between genres and categories are flexible and loosely defined, often with subgroups. Imagine is a graphic novel by author Alan Moore and artist Brett Hinds. It was first published by Eclipse as a hardcover in 1987. Between 1998 and 2008 the comic was reprinted by Plexxi, with Prelude (January 2003), Director's Cut (2004), and Super Fantastic (2005) editions. It reverted to a strict gorgebound format upon being issued by Necrogen Books in late 2008 with the print run exhaustively sold out by January 2009. In 2011, it was rereleased by Necrogen Books, with revised art and a 38-pages long adaptation of the final 48 pages. In intermittently 2015, it was reprinted by Action Ace, with a full-page art identification in the more recent issues of the Rogues art show publication. ""Imagine"" follows the life and career of Andy Warhol from 1969 to 1980. By loving Warhol, the two-story graphic novel resonates the anxiety and depression that latent in Warhol's generation. For a month in late January and early February 1977, Warhol was camera-shy due to gloomy, desolate material reality and depression. Although Margret Lipton falsely appraises him to be on top, Warhol is apprehensive when he sees Brutalist housing estate, Cosmic accidents, open sewers and gouged, desolate urban places. It describes Lennon and his family during an extended visit to New York City in early 1980 and their experiences at the funeral of friends Marilyn Monroe and Amy Carter (John's sister). They rejected him and two of his pretenders, but Arsaces, Mithridates and others were gathered by and taken into Parthia by Arsaces' daughter Rhodogune. After the death of Mithridates, Arsaces came back to Armenia and died there in 138. The Stand (comics) The Stand, published from 2008 to 2012, was a series of comic books by Marvel Comics based on Stephen King's novel of the same name. Based on the 1990 Complete & Uncut version of the novel, the comic adaptation was written by Roberto Aguirre-Sacasa with art by Mike Perkins and Laura Martin. Its thirty-one issues, divided into six story arcs, completed the telling of the unabridged novel. Stephen King served as Creative and Executive Director of the project.
Doc 2(Title: "The Stand") The Stand The Stand is a post-apocalyptic horror/fantasy novel by American author Stephen King. It expands upon the scenario of his earlier short story ""Night Surf"" and outlines the total breakdown of society after the accidental release of a strain of influenza that had been modified for biological warfare causes an apocalyptic pandemic, which kills off the majority of the world's human population. King dedicated the book to his wife, Tabitha: ""For Tabby: This dark chest of wonders."" Note that depending upon the edition of the novel, the dates of ""The Stand"" occur in either 1980–1981, 1985–1986, or 1990–1991. June

There is no near-contemporary evidence for his existence, the sources for his reign dating from the 13th and 14th centuries. Controversially, older Swedish historians have identified Eric with the legendary Swedish king ""Erik Weatherhat"" who is mentioned in some medieval king-lists as the predecessor of Eric the Victorious. Since the preceding king Anund is often identified with an Anund who flourished in the 840s and is mentioned by other sources (Rimbert and Adam of Bremen), ""Anundsson"" is probably the correct form of the patronym. Additive synthesis is the synthesis of sound (mostly electronic) using additive methods: taking the basic sine tone and using math and various techniques to create complex tones called harmonics that are then added as sine tones (hence additive synthesis). Only harmonics may be used. Adding simple tone together to create a tone has been used since the late 1800s. Claudio Monteverdi wrote the opening bits of ""Orfeo"" with sine tone player inserted into the sound. By the 20th century many composers had turned away from additive methods toward subtractive synthesis, a technique of removing frequency components, in part partly to achieve a wider range of new sound colorings. Composers turned to subtractive in some cases in order eliminate the physical ear-fatigue caused by obliterating the harmonics through extreme sine tones. Researchers then found that everything one hears including musical notes is made up of a frequency of one tone sounded and its harmonics. In 1946, a new way of hearing or sounding electronic music created through assigning the subtones of a basic note a unique frequency to produce a unique instrument or tone (trade secrets) were discovered. Dennis Stevens at IBM created this new technology. The new technology went through many iterations. Monteverdi used note values and larger increments of an octave of tone as the basis. An exactly corresponding theory was proposed during the 17th century by mathematician Marin Mersenne, where he explored vibrating strings Doc 2

<information>Doc 11 Novel Maugham's ""The Fall"": A Woman's Tragedy is a short story by Graham Greene, published under his own name in 1936 and collected in Now and Then in 1963. Maugham's warning against phoniness in women is strongly expressed in this short story. In the story, the author writes about an egoless, humorless and weak man, Walter Hartley. Walter has two kidneys removed without his knowledge. They are retained in a dead body, and when he learns about them, he sputters, turns very red and falls unconscious. The fall paperbound edition MAUGHAM’S ""FALL"". a woman's tragedy. The Fall is probably Maugham's darkest story. Its pessimism is extreme. The life of the woman it centers on seems without growth. She gives up her fantasy of ''happy ever after''—giving up the life of Gertrude Martin that just about everyone wants—to stay unmarried rather than be married to someone who doesn't love her—and to wait like Gertrude, out on the edge, in a useless life of degradedacceptance. The character of Ruth is portrayed as terribly lonely and somehow marooned in a world where she, like Gertrude, is barbarically abused and rejected. Because Ruth's needs are so innocent, it seems impossible that they would not find satisfaction. Unlike Gertrude, however, Ruth never fights for herself. The story is a potent summary of the Roman Catholic Justification. Walter, an eccentric and merry uncle of a friend of the Martin family, discovers fear and corpses while visiting the Maugham family. <information>Doc 1(Title: "Chrome (band)") Chrome (band) Chrome is an American rock band founded in San Francisco, California in 1975. The sound of the group is often coarse and features heavy elements of feedback and distortion. ""The King is dead, long live the King!"", or simply ""Long live the King!"", is a traditional proclamation made following the accession of a new monarch in various countries. The seemingly contradictory phrase is used to simultaneously announce the death of the previous monarch and assure the public of continuity by saluting the new monarch. In modern times, this phrase has become a popular phrasal template. <information>Doc 11 Time Time is all of the changes that are present at any moment: the ""here and now."" Time is an indicator of interval, preceded by and followed by other periods of time or events. It is finite as the speed of a clock increases as it gets closer to the speed of light, but never goes past it. In physics, the experience of time by an observer can be much slower or faster than a time that is measured by the observer, for example as measured by a remote clock. The obvious distinction between the phenomena is between the passing moment and the permanence of events, or states of motion. In much of modern physics—particularly in relativity—time is considered the thành viên inextricable from space that moves from the ""past"" to the ""future"". However, in some theories the actual existence of time is in question. The investigation of the passage of time is the scientific discipline of chronometry. The best-documented way that time appears to pass is through observation of stable periodic phenomena, such as atomic vibrations, pendulum swings, and heartbeat. Such phenomena are categorized, by the International System of Units (SI), as time standards, or more recently, frequency standards. A physicist using these measurements finds that precise measurements of time (frequency) are essential to its direct measurement since it undergoes what time does, change in an amount that depends on the relative motion of the measurer and the thing being measured. Some philosophers, particularly ancient philosophers and theologians, define time as any attribute or state of being of objects or systems that can be described by - or can be transformed into—one or more of the four dimensions (past/present/future, or top/bottom, left/right, front/back). These disciplines may assert, alternatively, that time can only be measured according to conventional standard models and
Those who dismiss this physical quality of time as being an artifact of our experience are postmodernists. To postmodernists, the perceived ordinariness of time is evidence that it is an inescapable effect of human language and consciousness. For postmodernists, when consciousness has migrated to proper personhood, time will become an illusion, a mere artifact of material reality. Against these postmodern concepts, there are traditionalist theories of time where time represents a dimension of being-qua-being where all other dimensions have their quantitative limits. A highway (also known as a motorway in the United Kingdom and Canada, and interstate highway in the United States and Philippines) is a tier of major roads carrying medium to heavy traffic traffic from one place to another, often in conjunction with freight movements.
There is a hierarchy of road classes around the world.
In common practice, highways may be used by motor vehicles only, but often heavy goods vehicles are prevented from using highways and are confined to what in some countries is known as a dual carriage-way system, in others a segregated system is used. Some states host a complex of highways, each dedicated to either all-moving traffic, mixed use or to car and light commercial users only, while others provide for a variety of different types of vehicles.
Highways are preferred routes for the movement of heavy commercial vehicles across a federation, especially if they will reach those vehicles’ destinations faster than other routes.
Only for such heavy goods vehicles (HGV) is there any reason to divide the highways into premium routes and general routes.

Mills also did screenwriting work that included screenplays for the Stars and Stripes films ""Speaks for Itself"" and ""Speechless."" <information>Doc 1(Title: "Christian mission") Christian mission A Christian mission is an organized effort to spread Christianity to new converts. Missions often involve sending individuals and groups, called missionaries, across boundaries, most commonly geographical boundaries, for the purpose of proselytism (conversion to Christianity, or from one Christian tradition to another). This involves evangelism (preaching a set of beliefs for the purpose of conversion), and humanitarian work, especially among the poor and disadvantaged. There are a few different kinds of mission trips: short-term, long-term, relational and ones meant simply for helping people in need. Some might choose to dedicate their whole lives to missions as well.
Doc 2(Title: Genre) Genre Genre () is any form or type of communication in any mode (written, spoken, digital, artistic, etc.) with socially-agreed upon conventions developed over time. Genre is most popularly known as a category of literature, music, or other forms of art or entertainment, whether written or spoken, audio or visual, based on some set of stylistic criteria, yet genres can be aesthetic, rhetorical, communicative, or functional. Genres form by conventions that change over time as cultures invent new genres and discontinue the use of old ones. Often, works fit into multiple genres by way of borrowing and recombining these conventions.
Doc 3(Title: "Adventure film") Adventure film Adventure films are a genre of film that typically use their action scenes to display and explore exotic locations in an energetic way. <information>Doc 1<information> from/under ground The English phrase from/to/under the hill means to sleep in a terrace or against a hillside. The phrase originates from the English Elizabethan era during the late 16th century. During that time, families inhabited smaller detached cottages in their communities, called homesteads or small plots of land, which were based on the grant of a partial honor feudalism. The traditional method of land division, also called ""old settlement"", was to own a smaller land-sized portion of a rectangular site that was on a small hilltop or in the upper ward against the hill. The surface of this small plot is called the courtyard, with its free or open land being called the terrace (old spelling: """") (E.g. in Liverpool, England). Although these conditions are becoming less common with the growth of populations and city growth, as these actions are always due, many people have placed a scheme of protecting the natural features of their land or city by using it as houses against the hillsides or terraces. The use of terraces is common not only to English tradition, but also to the cities of Indonesia (Gresik, Surakarta, Yogyakarta, Jakarta, Semarang, Tangerang), Brazil (Rio de Janeiro), Malaysia (Malacca), India (Hyderabad), Pakistan (Lahore), Bangladesh (Dhaka), Sri Lanka (Colombo), Laos (Vientiane), Nepal (Kathmandu), Uganda (Kampala), United States (New York, New Jersey), Philippines (Manila), Tanzania (Dar es Salaam), Morocco (Casablanca), South Korea (Paju), Hong Kong (Kowloon), and other Southeast Asia and Southeast Asian countries. Speak is the second studio album by the American indie rock band The Wallflowers, released on August 18, 1998 by Monument Records. This album marked the first entry on the band's name since medium length instrumental album ""The Best Part"" (1993). The Wallflowers' first featured guest was Leonardo DiCaprio, who also produced one track. The instrumentation on this album is an important part of it, as is the band's manic performances. Their energy can be heard through the song calls such as ""Swing It Across"" or ""Special Friend,"" and a particular dedication to the storytelling of the lyrics. The lead single, ""Get Up"", which became the band's first chart entry in its second decade was highly influential to 90's alternative/rap lyrics. ""Up All Night"" was another hit. Another track, ""Words"", features Daryl Hall, though the album was not released by Time Warner Records. The name of the band's second album originates from a song title in the included song, ""Speak Easy"".

The most general genres in literature are (in loose chronological order) epic, tragedy, comedy, and creative nonfiction. They can all be in the form of prose or poetry. <information>Doc 1(Title: "Attic style") Attic style In classical architecture, the term attic refers to a story or low wall above the cornice of a classical façade. The decoration of the topmost part of a building was particularly important in ancient Greek architecture and this came to be seen as typifying the ""Attica"" style, the earliest example known being that of the monument of Thrasyllus in Athens. It was largely employed in Ancient Rome, where their triumphal arches utilized it for inscriptions or for bas-relief sculpture. It was used also to increase the height of enclosure walls such as those of the Forum of Nerva.
Doc 2(Title: "Literary genre") Literary genre A literary genre is a category of literary composition. Genres may be determined by literary technique, tone, content, or even (as in the case of fiction) length. The distinctions between genres and categories are flexible and loosely defined, often with subgroups. The most general genres in literature are (in loose chronological order) epic, tragedy, comedy, and creative nonfiction. They can all be in the form of prose or poetry. Additionally, a genre such as satire, allegory or pastoral might appear in any of the above, not only as a subgenre (see below), but as a mixture of genres.

<information>Doc 1(Title: Genre) Genre Genre () is any form or type of communication in any mode (written, spoken, digital, artistic, etc.) with socially-agreed upon conventions developed over time. Genre is most popularly known as a category of literature, music, or other forms of art or entertainment, whether written or spoken, audio or visual, based on some set of stylistic criteria, yet genres can be aesthetic, rhetorical, communicative, or functional. Genres form by conventions that change over time as cultures invent new genres and discontinue the use of old ones. Often, works fit into multiple genres by way of borrowing and recombining these conventions.
Doc 2(Title: "Literary genre") Literary genre A literary genre is a category of literary composition. Genres may be determined by literary technique, tone, content, or even (as in the case of fiction) length. The distinctions between genres and categories are flexible and loosely defined, often with subgroups. The most general genres in literature are (in loose chronological order) epic, tragedy, comedy, and creative nonfiction. They can all be in the form of prose or poetry. Additionally, a genre such as satire, allegory or pastoral might appear in any of the above, not only as a subgenre (see below), but as a mixture of genres.
Doc 3(Title: "STEP Library") STEP Library STEP (Standard Template for Electronic Publishing) is a standard file format used to distribute Biblical software from various publishers to consumers wanting integrated text and reference works. STEP was conceived in 1995 by Craig Rairdin of Parsons Technology and Jim VanDuzer of Loizeaux Brothers Publishers. Rairdin and VanDuzer formed a consortium of Biblical software publishers called the Bible Software Industry Standards Group (BSISG) to oversee the development of the STEP specification and to develop common tools to be used by developers and publishers interested in implementing STEP compatibility in their programs or publishing books in the STEP format.</information>
